note: Verbatim phrases of the LOCKED narration, in spoken order, split at the script's <break> tags and sentence boundaries. Block windows were measured by FFT cross-correlation of each WC-01-03-sNN.mp3 against the joined master (8 kHz mono PCM, numpy rfft). The Σ(ffprobe audio+0.4) formula predicts 157.321s but the ffprobed master is 156.918s (0.403s off, past the ±0.3s gate). Cross-correlation shows the master is the 11 blocks concatenated with a uniform ~0.400s gap after each (peaks 3.4-4.7e12, all clean); the ffprobe per-file durations are inflated ~0.026-0.04s each by mp3 encoder-delay padding that the PCM concat strips, and the error accumulates to the 0.40s discrepancy. Each block startS is its measured onset; final end = ffprobed master duration 156.918s. The s08 cross-correlation peak at 96.640s confirms the LOCKED master contains the July-11 03:52 re-render of s08 (the 14/20/120 tile take), not the earlier blank-tile take.

phrases.text: WashCentral watches for inactivity.
Leave a session untouched and it does not drop you cold.
It warns you first, right over whatever page you left open.
Today that's the Command Center.
Watch the lower corner.
A small toast slides in: Session expiring soon, auto log-out in two minutes,
with a Stay Active button.
It's the compact version of the warning, easy to miss if you're looking away.
At the same moment, a dialog opens dead center.
Still there? it asks.
Two minutes remaining, counting down.
The line underneath tells you exactly how to stay: move your mouse or press any key.
Two buttons sit on the dialog.
Stay Logged In keeps you signed in and returns you to your work.
Log Out Now ends the session right away.
That one is a real logout, so leave it alone unless you mean to sign out.
So what if you ignore it?
At zero, the session closes and returns you to the sign-in page.
How long it may sit idle before the warning starts is set per user level,
and the exact figure for the demo login is still being pinned down.
Staying in is easy.
Move the mouse, tap a key, or click Stay Logged In.
The countdown stops, the dialog clears, and you pick up exactly where you were.
Stay Active on the toast does the same job.
Now, where does that timer come from?
Open Settings from the left rail and choose User Levels.
The breadcrumb reads Settings, then User Levels.
This page sets the idle limit for every level of user.
Across the top, three summary tiles.
Total Levels, Max Security Level, and Longest Timeout in minutes.
Today they read fourteen, twenty, and a hundred and twenty.
Fourteen levels, and the longest idle window runs two full hours.
The table has three columns.
Name, Security Level, and Session Timeout in minutes.
That last column is the one to know:
it's how many idle minutes a level gets before the Still there? countdown begins.
The numbers run from five minutes up to a hundred and twenty.
Cashiers and the lower levels sit at five;
administrators and service roles at a hundred and twenty.
So a cashier logged out after five idle minutes isn't hitting a bug.
That's the setting doing its job.
So when a manager says the system logs them out every few minutes, you know the move.
Open User Levels, find their level under Name, and read its Session Timeout.
A small number is the whole answer, and you never touch the row.
